figures.caption: Figure 3 Overview of NPO and AutoNPO. Right: NPO mechanism (§3.1): a near-future policy πF supplies a verified guidance trajectory o′x for each prompt, which replaces one slot of the on-policy rollout group whenever the current policy πθ is struggling on x. Left: AutoNPO (§3.3): online signals (reward stagnation, entropy drop, a mistake pool, a capability probe, and an estimated effective signal S) jointly decide when to intervene and how far to roll back.

Figure 2 Quality–stability trade-off and the effective learning signal S(∆). (a) For each source, the dot marks its position on these two axes, the surrounding shaded disk visualizes the magnitude of its variance cost V (larger disk, larger V ), and the triangle marks the portion of the trajectory quality that is effectively usable under the variance cost it induces. NPO targets the high-S region in the upper-right of the plane. (b) Q(∆) (blue) and V (∆) (red) measured on a vanilla GRPO run with Qwen3-VL-8B-Instruct: Q rises concavely while V grows exponentially. (c) The ratio S(∆) exhibits a clear interior maximum for both anchors T=0 and T=50, confirming the predicted U-shape.

Figure 4 Training dynamics of (Auto)NPO compared with vanilla GRPO. Red shaded regions in panels (a) and (b) highlight AutoNPO’s intervention windows. (a) Training reward dynamics: AutoNPO stays above GRPO throughout, with the gap widening step-wise after each intervention window. (b) Generation entropy: GRPO undergoes a steady entropy collapse, while AutoNPO’s interventions actively re-expand exploration and preserve a substantially higher-entropy policy. (c) IS-correction ablation on the late-stage intervention: the two NPO variants with and without exact importance-sampling correction both clearly outperform GRPO, indicating that IS correction is marginal for NPO. Thick lines are smoothed curves; thin lines show raw logged values.
Table 1 Multi-modal reasoning results on Qwen3-VL-8B. The blue rows are our NPO variants. Bold indicates the best result in each column. Avg. is the unweighted mean over the eight benchmarks.
